_meta.headTerm: malignant tumor of the main bronchus
_meta.authoringNotes: Cross-checked against NCCN NSCLC v3.2024, NCCN SCLC v1.2024, IASLC TNM 8th edition, USPSTF 2021 LDCT screening recommendation, PACIFIC (Antonia 2017 PMID 28885881), KEYNOTE-024 (Reck 2016 PMID 27718847), and WHO GLOBOCAN 2022 lung cancer statistics.

h1Title: Malignant Tumor of the Main Bronchus: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: A malignant tumor of the main bronchus (ICD-10: C34.0) is a centrally located lung cancer arising in the right or left main airway just beyond the carina, most often squamous cell carcinoma or small cell lung cancer (SCLC) and strongly linked to tobacco smoking in over 90% of cases. Lung cancer kills roughly 1.8 million people each year (WHO GLOBOCAN 2022), more than any other cancer worldwide. Central main-bronchus tumors classically present with chronic cough, blood-streaked sputum (hemoptysis), wheezing, recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia, and chest pain. Five-year survival depends sharply on stage: 60-70% for localized non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), 35% for regional disease, and 7% for distant metastasis (SEER 2014-2020). Modern treatment combines surgery, chemoradiation, molecularly targeted drugs, and PD-1/PD-L1 immunotherapy.

keyStats.prevalence: Lung cancer kills 1.8 million people per year globally (WHO GLOBOCAN 2022); central NSCLC and SCLC dominate hilar and main-bronchus disease
keyStats.demographics: Smokers and former smokers account for 80-90% of cases; median age at diagnosis 70 years (SEER)
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 2.5 million new lung cancers diagnosed worldwide in 2022 (GLOBOCAN)

definition: Malignant tumor of the main bronchus (ICD-10: C34.0) is a primary lung cancer originating in the lining of the right or left main bronchus, the large airway that branches from the trachea into each lung. Because the main bronchi sit centrally near the hilum, tumors here are categorized as central bronchogenic carcinomas and are histologically dominated by squamous cell carcinoma and small cell lung cancer, with large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma less common. Adenocarcinoma, the leading global lung cancer subtype overall, is more often peripheral than central. The tumor grows into the airway lumen and the surrounding peribronchial tissue, frequently invading mediastinal lymph nodes, the recurrent laryngeal nerve, esophagus, superior vena cava, or sympathetic chain. Staging follows the IASLC TNM 8th edition (T1-T4 by size and invasion, N0-N3 by nodal spread, M0-M1c by metastasis). Management is shared by pulmonologists, thoracic surgeons, medical oncologists, and radiation oncologists in a multidisciplinary tumor board, with molecular pathology (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, KRAS, NTRK, MET, RET, HER2) and PD-L1 immunohistochemistry now guiding systemic therapy in NSCLC. Bronchoscopy with biopsy or endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) provides the tissue diagnosis.

typesClassification.description: Arises from bronchial epithelium, classically central and tightly linked to smoking. Often cavitates and obstructs the airway, producing post-obstructive pneumonia and hemoptysis. Accounts for roughly 25-30% of NSCLC.

Highly aggressive neuroendocrine tumor, almost exclusively in smokers, typically central, and rapidly metastatic. Staged as limited-stage (confined to one hemithorax with regional nodes) or extensive-stage. Doubling time is short, and paraneoplastic syndromes are common.

typesClassification.type: Large cell carcinoma and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
typesClassification.description: Less common subtypes that can present centrally. Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma behaves clinically like SCLC; large cell carcinoma is treated along NSCLC lines.

typesClassification.type: Carcinoid tumor of the main bronchus
typesClassification.description: Neuroendocrine tumor with typical and atypical variants. Often endobronchial, may cause hemoptysis and obstruction, but generally less aggressive than SCLC and frequently curable with surgery alone.

primarySymptoms: Persistent cough lasting more than three weeks, often producing sputum, that changes character or worsens in a smoker is the single most common presenting symptom of central lung cancer.
Hemoptysis ranging from blood-streaked sputum to frank bleeding is reported by 20-50% of patients with central tumors and is more common in main-bronchus cancers than in peripheral lesions.
Unilateral wheezing or stridor that does not migrate between exams suggests fixed airway narrowing from an endobronchial tumor rather than asthma or bronchospasm.
Recurrent pneumonia confined to the same lobe should prompt urgent bronchoscopy, because a main-bronchus tumor obstructing drainage is the classic underlying cause.
Progressive shortness of breath on exertion, especially when accompanied by lobar collapse on chest X-ray, reflects post-obstructive atelectasis from luminal tumor growth.
Chest, shoulder, or interscapular pain develops when the tumor invades the chest wall, pleura, or mediastinal structures.
Hoarseness from recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy is most characteristic of left main-bronchus tumors, where the nerve loops under the aortic arch and is vulnerable to compression.
Horner's syndrome (ipsilateral ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis) signals invasion of the sympathetic chain by an apical or superior mediastinal tumor.
Superior vena cava syndrome with facial and upper-limb swelling, dilated chest-wall veins, and headache is most often caused by a right main-bronchus tumor compressing the SVC.
Dysphagia and weight loss appear when the tumor compresses or invades the adjacent esophagus, a late-stage feature in central disease.
Paraneoplastic syndromes are common: SCLC produces SIADH with hyponatremia and ectopic ACTH causing Cushing's syndrome, while squamous NSCLC can release PTHrP and produce hypercalcemia.
earlyWarningSigns: A new cough or a change in a long-standing smoker's cough that persists beyond three weeks
Any episode of blood in the sputum in a current or former smoker over age 40
Recurrent chest infections or pneumonia involving the same lobe within 6-12 months
Unintentional weight loss greater than 5% of body weight over six months in a smoker
Persistent fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance disproportionate to other medical conditions
emergencySigns: Massive hemoptysis (>100 mL in 24 hours or rapidly progressive bleeding) - airway-threatening; requires immediate hospitalization, bronchoscopy, and possible bronchial artery embolization
Stridor at rest, accessory muscle use, or oxygen saturation below 90% - signals critical central airway obstruction requiring urgent rigid bronchoscopy with stenting or debulking
Acute superior vena cava syndrome with severe facial swelling, altered mental status, or syncope - requires emergency imaging and urgent radiotherapy or SVC stenting
Sudden severe back pain with weakness or numbness in the legs - possible vertebral metastasis with spinal cord compression, an oncologic emergency
New focal neurological deficit, severe headache, or seizure - suggests brain metastasis; immediate brain MRI and dexamethasone are indicated

causes.description: Cigarette smoke is responsible for 80-90% of lung cancers overall and a higher share of central squamous and small cell tumors. Risk rises with pack-years; a 30-pack-year smoker has roughly 20-30 times the lung cancer risk of a never-smoker, and risk falls but does not vanish after quitting.

Chronic exposure raises lung cancer risk by 20-30% in never-smokers (US Surgeon General 2006). The carcinogens are the same nitrosamines, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, and aldehydes found in mainstream smoke.

Asbestos, silica, diesel exhaust, arsenic, chromium, nickel, beryllium, cadmium, and radon gas are established lung carcinogens. Asbestos combined with smoking multiplies risk by roughly 50-fold over baseline.

Indoor radon is the second leading cause of lung cancer in the United States after tobacco and accounts for an estimated 21,000 deaths per year (US EPA). Risk is highest in basement-level living spaces in radon-rich geology.
Fine particulate matter (PM2.5) is classified as a Group 1 lung carcinogen by IARC. Each 10 micrograms/cubic meter increase in long-term PM2.5 raises lung cancer mortality by roughly 8-14%.
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, pulmonary fibrosis, and prior tuberculosis scarring independently raise lung cancer risk through chronic epithelial injury and altered repair pathways.

Family history of lung cancer in a first-degree relative roughly doubles risk. Inherited variants in TP53, EGFR T790M germline mutation, and CYP1A1 polymorphisms influence carcinogen metabolism and DNA repair.

riskFactors.description: The dominant risk factor. Current smokers have 15-30x the risk of never-smokers; risk scales with pack-years and falls after quitting but remains elevated for at least 15 years.
USPSTF 2021 recommends annual low-dose CT screening in adults 50-80 with a 20-pack-year history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years.

Lung cancer rates have historically been higher in men, though female rates have risen sharply since the 1970s with changing smoking patterns. Squamous and small cell histologies remain more male-predominant.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational asbestos, silica, or diesel exhaust exposure
riskFactors.description: Construction, shipbuilding, mining, and heavy transport occupations carry elevated risk. Asbestos and smoking together multiply lung cancer risk roughly 50-fold.
Long-term radon levels above 4 pCi/L (EPA action level) raise lung cancer risk. Home radon testing is inexpensive and recommended in known geological hotspots.
A first-degree relative with lung cancer roughly doubles personal risk, independent of shared smoking exposure.
Patients with moderate-to-severe COPD have a 2-5x higher lung cancer incidence than smokers without COPD at equivalent pack-years.
Mantle radiotherapy for Hodgkin lymphoma or radiation for breast cancer raises later lung cancer risk, with peak excess incidence 10-30 years after exposure.
People living with HIV have roughly 2-3x the lung cancer incidence of the general population, partially independent of smoking rates.

affectedDemographics: Median age at diagnosis is 70 years (SEER); cases under age 40 are rare and often associated with EGFR or ALK mutations in non-smoking adenocarcinoma rather than central squamous histology
Worldwide, lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer death in both men and women (WHO GLOBOCAN 2022)

In China and parts of South Asia, indoor coal smoke and unventilated cooking fires contribute significantly to non-smoking lung cancer, more often peripheral adenocarcinoma than central squamous tumors

Black men in the United States have the highest lung cancer mortality of any demographic group despite lower average pack-years (CDC), reflecting differences in screening uptake, healthcare access, and biology
Small cell lung cancer has fallen as smoking rates have declined but still accounts for 13-15% of lung cancers and is almost never seen in lifelong never-smokers
diagnosisOverview: Workup of a suspected main-bronchus malignancy begins with a focused history (smoking pack-years, occupational exposures, weight loss, hemoptysis) and physical exam looking for clubbing, supraclavicular adenopathy, Horner's syndrome, and signs of SVC obstruction. Chest X-ray often shows a hilar mass, lobar collapse, or unresolving consolidation, but a normal film does not rule out central disease. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest with extension to the upper abdomen is the foundational imaging study, defining tumor size, airway involvement, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, vascular invasion, and adrenal or liver metastases. FDG PET-CT then maps metabolically active disease and detects distant metastases not visible on CT, sharpening clinical staging by 10-20% over CT alone. Tissue diagnosis is mandatory before any treatment: flexible bronchoscopy with forceps biopsy of an endobronchial lesion has a yield over 90% for centrally visible tumors, while endobronchial ultrasound with transbronchial needle aspiration (EBUS-TBNA) samples mediastinal lymph nodes for both diagnosis and staging. Brain imaging is part of routine staging - contrast-enhanced MRI is standard in NSCLC stages II and above, and mandatory at diagnosis in all SCLC because clinically silent brain metastases are common. For NSCLC, comprehensive molecular profiling (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, KRAS G12C, NTRK, MET exon 14, RET, HER2) and PD-L1 immunohistochemistry (22C3 antibody, tumor proportion score) are required to direct first-line systemic therapy. Pulmonary function testing and cardiopulmonary exercise testing assess fitness for surgical resection. Final staging follows the IASLC TNM 8th edition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard anatomic imaging for size, location, airway involvement, mediastinal nodes, and chest-wall or vascular invasion. Drives initial T and N staging.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-15 minute CT scan with iodinated contrast injected through an IV line. Patients hold their breath briefly during image acquisition. Results are typically available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects metabolically active tumor and distant metastases, refining clinical staging before treatment. Helps differentiate scar from active malignancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patients fast for 6 hours, receive an IV injection of FDG tracer, rest quietly for 60 minutes, and lie still for a 20-30 minute scan. Total visit time is 2-3 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization of the main bronchus and biopsy of endobronchial tumor for histologic diagnosis and molecular testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-40 minute outpatient procedure under conscious sedation or general anesthesia. A thin flexible scope passes through the nose or mouth into the airway. Sore throat and mild hoarseness for 1-2 days are common.

diagnosticTests.test: Endobronchial ultrasound with transbronchial needle aspiration (EBUS-TBNA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Samples mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes for both diagnosis and accurate N staging without surgical mediastinoscopy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed during the same bronchoscopy session under sedation. An ultrasound-tipped scope identifies enlarged nodes and a fine needle samples them in real time. Recovery is the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects intracranial metastases. Mandatory at diagnosis for all SCLC and recommended for NSCLC stages II-IV.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan with IV contrast. Patients lie still inside a magnetic tunnel; ear protection is provided for the loud knocking sounds. No radiation is involved.

diagnosticTests.test: Molecular and PD-L1 testing on tumor tissue
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies actionable driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, KRAS G12C, NTRK, MET, RET, HER2) and PD-L1 tumor proportion score that guide first-line therapy in advanced NSCLC.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in pathology from the existing biopsy sample. Results return in 7-14 days. No additional procedure is needed unless tissue is insufficient.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess preoperative lung function and predict postoperative FEV1 to determine whether lobectomy or pneumonectomy is safe.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry and diffusing capacity testing in a pulmonary lab, lasting 30-45 minutes. Patients perform several forced breaths into a mouthpiece while wearing a nose clip.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of a malignant main-bronchus tumor depends on histology (NSCLC vs SCLC), stage, molecular profile, and patient fitness, and is coordinated through a multidisciplinary tumor board. For resectable NSCLC stages I and II, anatomic lobectomy or pneumonectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection is the standard of care, with adjuvant chemotherapy considered for stage IB tumors over 4 cm and routinely given for stage II; 5-year survival is roughly 90% for stage IA, 73% for IB, and 65% for IIA. For locally advanced stage III NSCLC, concurrent platinum-based chemoradiotherapy followed by one year of durvalumab consolidation immunotherapy is the standard (PACIFIC trial, Antonia 2017 PMID 28885881), which extended 5-year overall survival to roughly 43%. Stage IV NSCLC management is molecularly driven: EGFR-mutant disease responds to osimertinib (5-year OS approximately 50% in advanced EGFR+ NSCLC), ALK-positive disease to alectinib or lorlatinib, ROS1 to entrectinib or crizotinib, BRAF V600E to dabrafenib plus trametinib, KRAS G12C to sotorasib or adagrasib. PD-L1 testing dictates first-line immunotherapy for non-driver tumors: pembrolizumab monotherapy when PD-L1 tumor proportion score is 50% or higher (KEYNOTE-024, Reck 2016 PMID 27718847), and chemotherapy plus pembrolizumab for lower PD-L1 expression. For SCLC, limited-stage disease receives concurrent platinum-etoposide chemoradiation plus prophylactic cranial irradiation, with 5-year survival of 25-30%. Extensive-stage SCLC is treated with platinum-etoposide plus atezolizumab or durvalumab maintenance (IMpower133, CASPIAN trials), modestly improving median overall survival to 12-13 months. Bronchoscopic palliation - laser, argon plasma coagulation, electrocautery, cryotherapy, or airway stenting - relieves obstructive symptoms, post-obstructive pneumonia, dyspnea, and hemoptysis in patients not curable by surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Platinum-doublet chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin plus etoposide, pemetrexed, paclitaxel, or vinorelbine)
medicalTreatments.description: Backbone of systemic therapy in both NSCLC and SCLC. Used as adjuvant therapy after resection, concurrently with radiotherapy in stage III NSCLC and limited-stage SCLC, and in palliative regimens for advanced disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates of 30-50% in advanced NSCLC and 60-80% in SCLC, though duration of response in SCLC is short.

medicalTreatments.name: Durvalumab consolidation immunotherapy (10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks for up to 12 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-PD-L1 antibody given after concurrent chemoradiation in unresectable stage III NSCLC. Establishes systemic immune surveillance and improves long-term survival (PACIFIC trial).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Five-year overall survival 42.9% vs 33.4% with placebo; progression-free survival nearly doubled (Antonia 2017, Spigel 2022 PACIFIC 5-year update).

medicalTreatments.name: Pembrolizumab monotherapy (200 mg IV every 3 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-PD-1 antibody used first-line in advanced NSCLC when PD-L1 tumor proportion score is 50% or higher and no actionable driver mutation is present.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival approximately 26.3 months vs 13.4 months with chemotherapy in KEYNOTE-024 (Reck 2016); 5-year OS 31.9% vs 16.3%.

medicalTreatments.name: Osimertinib (80 mg orally daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Third-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor for EGFR-mutant NSCLC. First-line in advanced disease and adjuvant after resection of stage IB-IIIA EGFR+ NSCLC (ADAURA trial).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median PFS 18.9 months and median OS 38.6 months in first-line advanced EGFR+ NSCLC (FLAURA); 88% reduction in disease recurrence as adjuvant therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Alectinib (600 mg orally twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-generation ALK inhibitor with strong CNS penetration. First-line therapy for ALK-positive advanced NSCLC.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median PFS 34.8 months vs 10.9 months with crizotinib (ALEX trial); 5-year OS 62.5%.

medicalTreatments.name: Sotorasib (960 mg orally daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Covalent KRAS G12C inhibitor for previously treated advanced NSCLC with the KRAS G12C mutation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective response rate 37%, median PFS 6.8 months, median OS 12.5 months in CodeBreaK 100.

medicalTreatments.name: Atezolizumab plus carboplatin and etoposide (extensive-stage SCLC)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line regimen for extensive-stage SCLC, with atezolizumab continued as maintenance after induction. Comparable regimen uses durvalumab (CASPIAN trial).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median OS 12.3 months vs 10.3 months with chemotherapy alone (IMpower133); statistically significant survival gain in a disease where progress had been minimal for decades.

medicalTreatments.name: Concurrent thoracic radiotherapy (60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions)
medicalTreatments.description: Given concurrently with chemotherapy in unresectable stage III NSCLC and limited-stage SCLC. Stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) is an alternative curative option for medically inoperable stage I NSCLC.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SBRT achieves 3-year local control above 90% in stage I NSCLC; concurrent chemoradiation provides durable disease control as the platform for durvalumab consolidation.

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI, 25 Gy in 10 fractions)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard for limited-stage SCLC that has responded to chemoradiation. Reduces brain metastasis risk and improves survival.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 3-year brain metastasis incidence from approximately 59% to 33% and improves 3-year overall survival by roughly 5% (Auperin meta-analysis).

surgicalOptions.name: Lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection
surgicalOptions.description: Standard curative resection for stage I-II NSCLC fit enough for surgery. Increasingly performed via video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) or robotic approaches with reduced morbidity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year overall survival approximately 90% for stage IA and 73% for stage IB after complete resection.

surgicalOptions.name: Pneumonectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of an entire lung, reserved for central tumors invading the main bronchus where lobectomy or sleeve resection cannot achieve clear margins. Carries higher operative mortality than lobectomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Operative mortality 3-7%; long-term survival depends on stage but is comparable to lobectomy stage-for-stage when margins are clear.

surgicalOptions.name: Sleeve lobectomy or bronchoplastic resection
surgicalOptions.description: Removes the tumor-bearing segment of main or lobar bronchus and reconnects the remaining airway, preserving lung tissue and avoiding pneumonectomy in selected central tumors.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year survival comparable to standard lobectomy; lung function preservation superior to pneumonectomy with anastomotic complication rates of 3-5%.

surgicalOptions.name: Mediastinoscopy or VATS lymph node staging
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical sampling of mediastinal nodes when EBUS-TBNA is inconclusive but accurate N staging is critical to treatment decisions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sensitivity of cervical mediastinoscopy approximately 80% for N2/N3 disease with morbidity under 2%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bronchoscopic palliation (laser, argon plasma coagulation, electrocautery, cryotherapy, airway stenting)
alternativeTreatments.description: Endoscopic debulking of obstructing endobronchial tumor and placement of silicone or metallic airway stents to relieve dyspnea, post-obstructive pneumonia, and hemoptysis in patients not curable by surgery. Provides rapid symptom improvement in 80-90% of selected cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation support (varenicline, nicotine replacement, behavioral counseling)
alternativeTreatments.description: Quitting after a lung cancer diagnosis improves treatment tolerance, reduces second primary cancers, and lowers cardiovascular and pulmonary mortality. Even at diagnosis it confers meaningful survival benefit (Parsons 2010 meta-analysis).

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured exercise and education programs before surgery (prehabilitation) and after treatment improve fitness, reduce postoperative complications, and improve quality of life in lung cancer survivors.

whySeeSpecialist: Any suspected main-bronchus cancer requires evaluation in a multidisciplinary thoracic oncology program that includes a pulmonologist trained in advanced bronchoscopy, a thoracic surgeon, a medical oncologist, a radiation oncologist, and a thoracic pathologist. Treatment plans built in a tumor board setting consistently achieve better staging accuracy, higher rates of curative-intent therapy, and improved survival compared with single-specialist management. Urgent referral is indicated for hemoptysis in a smoker, recurrent same-lobe pneumonia, unexplained weight loss with cough, hoarseness with a hilar mass, or any suspicious finding on chest CT.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a thoracic oncology team at a high-volume cancer center with prospective tumor-board review, EBUS-TBNA capability, molecular pathology for full NSCLC biomarker testing, robotic or VATS surgical expertise, and stereotactic radiotherapy. Ask about the center's annual lung cancer surgical volume (above 50 cases per year correlates with better outcomes), access to clinical trials, and integrated palliative care. Continuity of an oncology nurse navigator simplifies a complex multi-month treatment pathway.
preventionStrategies: Never start smoking, and quit at any age - within 10 years of quitting, lung cancer risk falls by 30-50% compared with continued smoking (US Surgeon General 2020)
Avoid secondhand smoke at home and in workplaces; advocate for smoke-free public-space laws which lower community lung cancer incidence over 10-15 years
Test home radon levels and mitigate if levels exceed 4 pCi/L (EPA action level) using sub-slab depressurization or ventilation
Use appropriate respiratory protection and follow exposure limits for asbestos, silica, diesel exhaust, and welding fume in industrial and construction work
Pursue annual low-dose CT screening if you are 50-80 years old with a 20-pack-year smoking history and currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years (USPSTF 2021)
Treat COPD and pulmonary infections to limit chronic airway inflammation, which independently raises lung cancer risk
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking with combined pharmacotherapy (varenicline or combination NRT) and behavioral support - quit rates roughly triple compared with willpower alone
Limit alcohol intake; heavy alcohol use compounds tobacco-related cancer risk and worsens chemotherapy tolerance
Maintain regular aerobic and resistance exercise during and after treatment to preserve pulmonary function, lean mass, and quality of life
Eat a Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fruits, whole grains, fish, and olive oil; high intake of cured and processed meat is associated with higher lung cancer mortality
Stay current with influenza, COVID-19, and pneumococcal vaccinations - infections are a leading cause of treatment interruption and hospitalization
Track symptoms (cough, hemoptysis, breathlessness, weight) in a simple diary and bring it to oncology visits

recommended: Vegetables and fruits providing at least 5 servings daily, with a focus on cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cabbage, kale)
Whole grains, legumes, and nuts as primary carbohydrate and protein sources
Fatty fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) two to three times per week for omega-3 intake
Adequate protein (1.0-1.5 grams/kg/day) during chemotherapy to preserve muscle mass
At least 2 liters of fluid daily unless restricted; small frequent meals during chemotherapy if appetite is poor
Lean dairy or fortified alternatives for calcium and vitamin D maintenance
avoid: Tobacco in all forms - smoked, chewed, or vaped
High-dose beta-carotene supplements - randomized trials (ATBC, CARET) showed increased lung cancer mortality in smokers taking high-dose beta-carotene
Processed and cured meats, which are classified as Group 1 carcinogens by IARC
Heavy alcohol intake; restrict to one drink daily for women and two for men, less during chemotherapy
Unwashed raw produce during neutropenic phases of chemotherapy to reduce infection risk
Crash dieting or fasting during active treatment - malnutrition worsens outcomes

exerciseGuidelines: Pulmonary rehabilitation and structured prehabilitation before lung surgery improve postoperative outcomes and shorten hospital stay. After diagnosis, aim for 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic exercise (walking, stationary cycling) plus two sessions of resistance training when tolerated. Use supplemental oxygen during exercise if hypoxic. Rest during acute treatment-related fatigue, but avoid prolonged bed rest, which accelerates muscle loss.
dailyManagement: Take oral targeted therapy or immunotherapy supportive medications at the same time each day; missed doses materially affect TKI exposure
Monitor for treatment toxicity - rash and diarrhea with EGFR inhibitors, transaminitis with ALK inhibitors, thyroiditis and pneumonitis with immunotherapy
Check temperature daily during chemotherapy cycles and contact the oncology team for any fever above 38.0 degrees Celsius (neutropenic fever is an emergency)
Use prescribed antiemetics on schedule, not just when nauseated; prophylactic dosing prevents treatment-limiting nausea
Maintain hydration and skin care; immunotherapy and EGFR inhibitor rashes respond best to early treatment
Keep an up-to-date list of all medications, including supplements, to share with every provider
prognosis: Stage at diagnosis is the dominant prognostic factor. For NSCLC: 5-year survival is approximately 65% for localized disease, 37% for regional spread, and 9% for distant metastasis (SEER 2014-2020), with stage IA reaching 90% after complete resection. The PACIFIC regimen has transformed stage III NSCLC, raising 5-year overall survival to roughly 43% from less than 20% a decade ago. Advanced molecularly defined NSCLC has seen unprecedented gains: 5-year survival in EGFR-mutant advanced disease treated with osimertinib approaches 50%, and ALK-positive disease on alectinib or lorlatinib shows median PFS over 30 months. Small cell lung cancer remains more difficult: limited-stage 5-year survival is 25-30% with chemoradiation and PCI; extensive-stage median survival is 12-13 months with chemoimmunotherapy. Active smoking at diagnosis worsens every prognostic category by 10-30%, while quitting at diagnosis confers a meaningful survival benefit even in advanced disease. Tumor genotype, performance status, response to first-line therapy, and access to clinical trials all influence individual outcomes beyond stage alone.
recoveryTimeline: After lobectomy, most patients leave hospital in 3-5 days and return to baseline activity within 4-6 weeks; pulmonary function typically recovers to 80-90% of preoperative values within 3-6 months. Concurrent chemoradiation for stage III NSCLC runs 6-7 weeks of treatment followed by 12 months of durvalumab; esophagitis and fatigue peak around week 4 and resolve by 6-8 weeks after radiation ends. SCLC chemoradiation typically completes in 4-6 cycles over 4-5 months. Targeted therapy and immunotherapy are continued indefinitely until progression or intolerable toxicity, with patients commonly remaining on treatment for years in molecularly driven NSCLC. Surveillance imaging is performed every 3-6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6-12 months, with the highest recurrence risk in the first 2 years after curative treatment.
complications: Massive hemoptysis from tumor erosion into bronchial or pulmonary arteries - airway-threatening and requires emergency bronchial artery embolization or rigid bronchoscopy
Post-obstructive pneumonia and lung abscess distal to the tumor, often recurrent and difficult to clear without addressing the obstruction
Superior vena cava syndrome with facial swelling, dilated chest veins, and headache - relieved by stenting or urgent radiotherapy
Recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy producing hoarseness and aspiration risk, especially with left main-bronchus tumors
Spinal cord compression from vertebral metastasis - an oncologic emergency requiring high-dose steroids, MRI within hours, and urgent decompressive radiotherapy or surgery
Pneumonitis from radiotherapy or checkpoint immunotherapy - presents with dry cough, dyspnea, and ground-glass opacities; treated with steroids and treatment hold
Brain metastases producing focal neurological deficits, seizures, or cognitive change - more common in SCLC and EGFR/ALK NSCLC

supportResources.name: American Lung Association - Lung Cancer Hub
supportResources.description: Patient education, helpline (1-800-LUNGUSA), and treatment navigation resources.

supportResources.name: LUNGevity Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient-focused organization with biomarker testing advocacy, support groups, and clinical trial matching.

supportResources.name: GO2 for Lung Cancer
supportResources.description: Patient helpline, screening advocacy, and survivor community resources.

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute - Lung Cancer (PDQ)
supportResources.description: Authoritative US government source for evidence-based lung cancer information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia is an acute infection that responds to antibiotics within 7-14 days, while a main-bronchus tumor often presents as recurrent pneumonia in the same lobe that fails to fully clear. Any pneumonia that recurs in the same location or fails to resolve on follow-up imaging warrants bronchoscopy to rule out obstructing tumor.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD is a diffuse, progressive airflow obstruction with bilateral wheezing and emphysematous changes, whereas a main-bronchus tumor produces fixed unilateral wheezing or stridor and a focal hilar mass on imaging. COPD raises lung cancer risk, so the two frequently coexist, and a new focal symptom in a COPD patient should prompt CT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes reversible bilateral airflow obstruction that responds to bronchodilators, with peak flow improving after inhaler use. A central bronchial tumor produces fixed, unilateral wheezing that does not reverse with bronchodilators and is paired with imaging abnormalities. New-onset wheezing in an adult smoker warrants imaging before an asthma label.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculosis classically produces upper-lobe cavities, weight loss, night sweats, and a positive sputum acid-fast bacilli smear or NAAT. Squamous main-bronchus tumors can also cavitate and cause weight loss, so imaging plus sputum AFB and biopsy distinguish them. The two can coexist, particularly in high-TB-prevalence regions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mesothelioma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mesothelioma arises from pleural mesothelial cells, typically presents with a unilateral pleural effusion and rind-like pleural thickening, and is strongly linked to asbestos exposure rather than smoking. A main-bronchus tumor originates in the airway lumen and is dominated by smoking-related squamous or small cell histology.

relatedConditions.name: Lung Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Main-bronchus malignancies are a specific anatomic subset of lung cancer, dominated by central squamous and small cell histologies; general lung cancer overview applies.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: COPD and central lung cancer share tobacco etiology and frequently coexist; COPD raises lung cancer risk independently and complicates surgical candidacy.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Recurrent pneumonia in the same lobe is a classic presenting picture of an obstructing main-bronchus tumor and must trigger CT and bronchoscopy.

relatedConditions.name: Laryngeal Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares tobacco etiology and squamous cell histology; synchronous or metachronous head-and-neck primaries occur in 5-10% of smokers with main-bronchus cancer.

relatedConditions.name: Mesothelioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both occur in patients with thoracic carcinogen exposure; mesothelioma involves the pleura rather than the airway and is asbestos-driven.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Prior TB scarring is a recognized lung cancer risk factor and the two can coexist in endemic regions, requiring careful differentiation on imaging and biopsy.

faqs.question: What is a malignant tumor of the main bronchus?
faqs.answer: A malignant tumor of the main bronchus (ICD-10 C34.0) is a primary lung cancer in the right or left main airway. It is usually squamous cell or small cell carcinoma, smoking-linked, and causes cough, blood-streaked sputum, wheezing, and recurrent same-lobe pneumonia.

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of a main bronchus tumor?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms include a new or changed cough lasting over three weeks, blood in the sputum, unilateral wheezing not relieved by inhalers, repeated same-lobe pneumonia, breathlessness, hoarseness, and unintentional weight loss. Any of these in a current or former smoker over 40 warrants chest CT.

faqs.question: How is a main bronchus tumor diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with contrast chest CT and PET-CT for staging, then flexible bronchoscopy with biopsy and EBUS-TBNA to sample mediastinal lymph nodes. Brain MRI is added for stage II+ NSCLC and is mandatory in all SCLC. Molecular and PD-L1 testing on the biopsy guide systemic therapy.

faqs.question: Is a main bronchus tumor always cancer?
faqs.answer: Most main-bronchus masses in adult smokers are malignant, but benign endobronchial lesions occur (hamartomas, lipomas, papillomas) along with low-grade carcinoid tumors. Tissue biopsy is the only reliable way to tell them apart, so the workup is imaging followed by bronchoscopy.

faqs.question: What causes cancer of the main bronchus?
faqs.answer: Tobacco smoking causes 80-90% of cases. Other contributors include secondhand smoke, radon, asbestos, silica, diesel exhaust, outdoor air pollution, prior thoracic radiotherapy, chronic lung disease such as COPD or pulmonary fibrosis, and family history of lung cancer.

faqs.question: How is a main bronchus tumor treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on stage and histology. Early NSCLC: lobectomy or sleeve resection. Stage III NSCLC: chemoradiation plus 12 months of durvalumab. Stage IV NSCLC: targeted therapy or immunotherapy. SCLC: platinum-etoposide with radiation and immunotherapy.

faqs.question: Can a main bronchus tumor be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes when caught early. Stage IA NSCLC reaches roughly 90% five-year survival after resection. Stage III NSCLC treated with chemoradiation plus durvalumab achieves about 43% five-year survival. Stage IV is rarely cured but can be controlled for years. Limited-stage SCLC is cured in 25-30% of cases.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for main bronchus cancer?
faqs.answer: Five-year relative survival for NSCLC is roughly 65% for localized disease, 37% for regional spread, and 9% for distant metastasis (SEER 2014-2020). Limited-stage SCLC five-year survival is 25-30%; extensive-stage median survival is 12-13 months with chemoimmunotherapy.

faqs.question: Does quitting smoking after diagnosis help?
faqs.answer: Yes. Quitting at diagnosis improves chemotherapy tolerance, lowers postoperative complications, reduces second cancers, and improves overall survival even in advanced disease. A 2010 meta-analysis showed roughly doubled five-year survival in early-stage patients who quit.

faqs.question: Who should be screened for lung cancer with low-dose CT?
faqs.answer: The USPSTF (2021) recommends annual low-dose CT screening for adults aged 50-80 with a 20 pack-year smoking history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years. Screening reduces lung cancer mortality by roughly 20% based on the National Lung Screening Trial.

faqs.question: What does coughing up blood mean in a smoker?
faqs.answer: Hemoptysis in a current or former smoker is a red-flag symptom. Causes range from bronchitis and pneumonia to bronchiectasis and lung cancer. Any episode of blood-streaked sputum in a smoker over 40 warrants chest CT. Massive hemoptysis (over 100 mL in 24 hours) is a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What is the difference between small cell and non-small cell lung cancer?
faqs.answer: Small cell lung cancer is a fast-growing neuroendocrine tumor linked to smoking, treated with chemotherapy, radiation, and immunotherapy. Non-small cell lung cancer includes adenocarcinoma, squamous, and large cell types, treated by surgery early and targeted therapy in advanced stages.

faqs.question: Can main bronchus cancer be removed by surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes when the tumor is localized and the patient is fit. Options include lobectomy, sleeve lobectomy or bronchoplastic resection to preserve lung tissue, and pneumonectomy when complete removal requires taking the whole lung. Mediastinal lymph node dissection accompanies the resection.

faqs.question: What is bronchoscopic palliation?
faqs.answer: Bronchoscopic palliation uses laser, argon plasma coagulation, electrocautery, cryotherapy, or stents to reopen an airway blocked by tumor. It relieves breathlessness, treats post-obstructive pneumonia, and controls bleeding. Symptom improvement occurs in 80-90% of selected cases.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for main bronchus cancer take?
faqs.answer: Curative treatment usually spans several months. Lobectomy needs a 3-5 day hospital stay and 4-6 week recovery; adjuvant chemo adds 3-4 months. Stage III chemoradiation runs 6-7 weeks plus 12 months durvalumab. Targeted therapy continues until progression or toxicity.

faqs.question: What is the PACIFIC regimen?
faqs.answer: The PACIFIC regimen is concurrent platinum-based chemoradiotherapy followed by one year of durvalumab consolidation immunotherapy for unresectable stage III NSCLC. The PACIFIC trial (Antonia 2017) improved five-year overall survival from 33.4% with placebo to 42.9% with durvalumab.

faqs.question: Can lung cancer occur in non-smokers?
faqs.answer: Yes. Roughly 10-20% of lung cancers occur in lifelong never-smokers, more often adenocarcinoma than central squamous or small cell tumors. Causes include secondhand smoke, radon, occupational carcinogens, and air pollution. EGFR and ALK mutations are more common in non-smokers.

faqs.question: What are paraneoplastic syndromes in lung cancer?
faqs.answer: Paraneoplastic syndromes are systemic effects from hormones released by the tumor. SCLC commonly produces SIADH with hyponatremia and ectopic ACTH causing Cushing's syndrome. Squamous NSCLC can release PTHrP and cause hypercalcemia. They sometimes appear before the tumor itself is found.

faqs.question: What is the role of immunotherapy?
faqs.answer: PD-1 and PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, atezolizumab, durvalumab, nivolumab) let T cells attack tumor cells. They are standard first-line therapy for advanced NSCLC without driver mutations and for extensive-stage SCLC, improving long-term survival in PD-L1 high disease.

faqs.question: Is genetic testing of the tumor important?
faqs.answer: Yes for NSCLC. Profiling for EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, KRAS, NTRK, MET, RET, and HER2 is required because matched targeted therapy roughly doubles survival compared with chemotherapy in advanced disease. PD-L1 immunohistochemistry guides immunotherapy selection and should precede first-line treatment.

faqs.question: What follow-up is needed after treatment?
faqs.answer: After curative treatment, surveillance includes contrast chest CT every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months up to 5 years, with clinical visits each time. Recurrence risk peaks in the first 2 years. Smoking cessation and pulmonary rehabilitation continue lifelong.

metaDescription: Main bronchus cancer (ICD-10 C34.0): central lung tumor causing cough, hemoptysis, and wheezing. Diagnosis, staging, surgery, immunotherapy, and prognosis.
